content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


March 19, 2002

Larissa M. Seto, Asst. City Attorney
City of Pleasanton
Post Office Box 520
Pleasanton, CA 94566-0802

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-217

Dear Ms. Seto:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Jon Harvey regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

Which of the following create an economic interest for Jon Harvey?

1. Stock and equity holdings of a non-profit foundation for which he is a director;

2. The corporation owned by him;

2a. The investments of the corporation;

2b. Income from his corporation's investments;

3. A private annuity with an individual.


¹ Government Code §§ 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, §§ 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-01-217
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. Mr. Harvey has an economic interest in the nonprofit as a source of income,
but does not have an economic interest in the investments of the nonprofit.

2. Assuming the investment in the corporation held by him and his wife is valued
at $2,000 or more, Mr. Harvey has an economic interest in his corporation.

2a. Mr. Harvey has an economic interest in any investments of the
corporation which are valued at $2,000 or more.

2b. If the amount of income which is attributed to him from one source
amounts to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the time a relevant decision is
made, he will have an economic interest in this source. See Analysis for method
of calculating amount of income received.

3. Mr. Harvey does not have an investment interest in the annuity. If the amount
of annuity payments made by the individual to Mr. Harvey totals $500 or more within 12
months prior to the time a relevant decision is made, Mr. Harvey will have an economic
interest in the individual.

FACTS

Jon Harvey, Planning Commissioner for the City of Pleasanton, is one of two
directors of a non-profit 501(c)(3) foundation. The foundation's activities include
strengthening families and preserving wildlife habitat. The foundation owns several
hundred stocks and equities as its endowment. These assets, as well as the income or loss
generated from them, pay for foundation activities and expenses. As a director of the
foundation, Mr. Harvey does not currently receive any salary. The foundation does
provide Mr. Harvey with medical and dental benefits, and reimburses him for out-of-
pocket expenses related to his work as a director.

The foundation's stocks and equities are traded and monitored by a certified
consultant (Certified Investment Manager Analyst) retained by the foundation. The
consultant executes the investment plan created in cooperation with Mr. Harvey. As
such, the consultant hires the appropriate portfolio money managers who exercise their
independent judgment in deciding which stocks and equities to buy, hold or sell on
behalf of the foundation. Over a thousand transactions occur on behalf of the foundation
each year based upon the actions of the portfolio managers.

As a director of the foundation, Mr. Harvey technically has some discretion to
direct the foundation's consultant to take specific action regarding a stock or equity.
However, as a practical matter, Mr. Harvey has neither taken an active role in directing
the foundation's consultant, nor does Mr. Harvey regularly monitor which stocks and
equities are actually held by the foundation.

File No. A-01-217
Page No. 3

Mr. Harvey owns a 50% interest in a corporation in which his spouse owns the
other 50% interest. Mr. Harvey is an officer of the corporation. The corporation's assets
are managed by an investment manager who hires several portfolio money managers who
invest in hundreds of different stocks, fixed income instruments, real estate and managed
futures. The portfolio money managers exercise their independent judgment when
deciding which stocks and equities to buy, sell and hold for the corporation. Stock
transactions occur daily and number in the thousands over the course of a year.

Mr. Harvey has the ability to direct the investment manager to buy, hold or sell
specific stocks and equities with funds that are not managed by portfolio money
managers.

Mr. Harvey is an annuitant. The annuity is a private annuity with an individual.
The assets of the annuity are managed by a consultant who hires other portfolio money
managers who invest in hundreds of different individual stocks, fixed income
instruments, real estate and managed futures. The portfolio money managers exercise
their independent judgment when deciding which stock and equities to buy, sell and hold
for the parent company. Stock transactions occur daily and number in the thousands over
the course of a year.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001.)
Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard analysis
for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given
governmental decision.² (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).) However, your questions concern
only the identification of Mr. Harvey's economic interests, step three of this analysis.

What are Mr. Harvey's economic interests?

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:

² This analysis is summarized in the Commission document entitled "Can I Vote? Conflict-of-
Interest Overview" (enclosed).

File No. A-01-217
Page No. 4

•    A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment 3 of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a);
Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d);
Regulation 18703.1(b));

•    A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2);

•    A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (Section 87103(c), Regulation 18703.3);

•    A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $320 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4);

•    A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5).

Foundation

     "Income" is "a payment received" and includes, in part, any salary, wage, or
advance. (Section 82030(a).) However, income does not include:

     "Salary 4 and reimbursement for expenses or per diem
received from a state, local, or federal government agency
and reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem
received from a bona fide nonprofit entity exempt from
taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue
Code." (Section 82030(b)(2)) (Footnote added.)

     Under section 82030(b), only "reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem
received from a bona fide nonprofit entity" are excluded from the definition of "income."

3    An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family" is defined
at Section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.

4    "Salary" from a state, local, or federal government agency means any and all payments made by a
government agency to a public official, or accrued to the benefit of a public official, as consideration for
the public official's services to the government agency. Such payments include wages, fees paid to
consultants who are public officials, pension benefits, health and other insurance coverage, rights to
compensated vacation and leave time, free or discounted transportation, payment or indemnification of
legal defense costs, and similar benefits." (Regulation 18232(a).)

File No. A-01-217
Page No. 5

Therefore, salary and other payments, including medical and dental benefits, received by
a public official from a nonprofit entity constitute income under the Act. As a result, Mr.
Harvey has an economic interest in the 501(c)(3) foundation for which he serves as a
director.

Since it is the nonprofit entity rather than Mr. Harvey that owns the stock and
equity holdings, Mr. Harvey does not have an economic interest in these holdings.

Corporation

In determining Mr. Harvey's investment in the corporation owned by him and his
spouse, please note that Mr. Harvey's investment for purposes of the Act includes a direct
or indirect investment. For purposes of the Act's conflict-of-interest rules, indirect
investment or interest means, in part, any investment or interest owned by the spouse or
dependent child of a public official. (Section 87103.) Therefore, assuming the
investments made by him and his wife amount to $2,000 or more, Mr. Harvey has an
economic interest in this corporation.

Corporate investments: "Investments of an individual include a pro rata share of
investments of any business entity, mutual fund, or trust in which the individual or
immediate family owns, directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or
greater." (Section 82034.) Since Mr. Harvey and his wife jointly own 100% of the
corporation, Mr. Harvey has an economic interest in any investments of the corporation
which amount to $2,000 or more.

Income from the Corporation: "'Income' of an individual also includes a pro rata
share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse
owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10% interest or greater." (Section 82030(a).)
Under the Act, an official's income includes a community property interest in the income
of his or her spouse. (Section 82030(a).) Mr. Harvey has a 50% interest in the
corporation as does his spouse. Therefore, 50% of the income from a particular source
will be attributed to him plus his community property interest in (half of) his spouse's
income. (King Advice Letter, No. I-99-145, Romney Advice Letter, No. A-99-263.) As a
result, he is attributed with 75% of income to the corporation from a particular source. If
the amount of income from one source amounts to $500 or more within 12 months prior
to the time a relevant decision is made, he will have an economic interest in this source.
Practically speaking, Mr. Harvey will have an economic interest in any person from
whom his corporation receives income totaling $667 or more during the pertinent time
period.⁵

Mr. Harvey will have an economic interest in such a source regardless of whether
the corporation's investment is handled by a portfolio money manager or investment
manager for the corporation.

___________________________
⁵ This situation occurs because 75% of $667 equals approximately $500.

File No. A-01-217
Page No. 6

Annuitant Investments

In the Elmore Opinion, the Commission determined that "[s]ection 82034
excludes insurance policies and time and demand deposits in a financial institution from
the definition of investment. Thus, ... holdings in annuities and savings bank deposits are
not "investments" under the Act." (In re Elmore (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 8.) Therefore, the
annuity held by Mr. Harvey does not constitute an "investment" for purposes of the Act.

Additionally, the Elmore Opinion analyzed whether the annuities, where sums
were withheld from a state employee's salary by the State of California pursuant to the
Public Employee's Retirement System plan or to various deferred compensation plan
options, constituted "income." Because the Act specifically excludes from the definition
of "income" salary received from state or local government, the earnings from those
annuities were determined not to be "income."

You have provided facts indicating that Mr. Harvey holds a private annuity with
an individual. Consequently, the government salary exception applicable in the Elmore
Opinion does not apply to Mr. Harvey's annuity. Therefore, under the definition of
"income," any payments received from the private annuity constitute income. If the
amount of these payments totals $500 or more within 12 months prior to the time a
relevant decision is made, Mr. Harvey will have an economic interest in that individual.

We note that you have provided information regarding only these economic
interests of Mr. Harvey. For purposes of this letter, we assume that he has no other
potential economic interests relevant to decisions before the City of Pleasanton Planning
Commission.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: [signature]
Natalie Bocangdra
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures
NB:jg
I:\AdviceLtrs\01-217

6 Please bear in mind that this letter is based on the facts you have presented to us. The
Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

3. Mr. Harvey does not have an investment interest in the annuity. If the amount

interest in the individual.

QUESTION: Which of the following create an economic interest for Jon Harvey?
1. Stock and equity holdings of a non-profit foundation for which he is a director;
2. The corporation owned by him;
2a. The investments of the corporation;
2b. Income from his corporation's investments;
3. A private annuity with an individual.

18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.

CONCLUSION: 1. Mr. Harvey has an economic interest in the nonprofit as a source of income,
but does not have an economic interest in the investments of the nonprofit.
2. Assuming the investment in the corporation held by him and his wife is valued
at $2,000 or more, Mr. Harvey has an economic interest in his corporation.
2a. Mr. Harvey has an economic interest in any investments of the
corporation which are valued at $2,000 or more.
2b. If the amount of income which is attributed to him from one source
amounts to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the time a relevant decision is
made, he will have an economic interest in this source. See Analysis for method
of calculating amount of income received.
3. Mr. Harvey does not have an investment interest in the annuity. If the amount
of annuity payments made by the individual to Mr. Harvey totals $500 or more within 12
months prior to the time a relevant decision is made, Mr. Harvey will have an economic
interest in the individual.
